q: The ray approximation of geometrical optics is valid for the study of interference and diffraction.
Huygens' principle states that every point on a wavefront may be considered as a source of secondary wavelets.
Secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with the speed $v$.
A wavefront is defined as a surface associated with a propagating wave which is a locus of all points in the wave that have the same phase.
Wavefront is always parallel to the direction of propagation.
The spherical wavefront from a source transforms into a plane wavefront when it is close to the source.
The angular position of a point of a periodic wave is known as the phase angle.
The phase angle is one of the important characteristics of the periodic wave.
If phase difference is $\pi, 3\pi, 5\pi$, these two positions are in phase.
Path difference indicates the distance between two points measured along the direction of propagation of the wave through the medium.
If path difference is $0, \lambda, 2\lambda, 3\lambda$, it is said in phase.
The relation between phase difference and path difference is expressed as $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$.
Wave interference is the phenomenon that occurs when two waves meet while traveling along the same medium.
Interference of light waves is defined as the modification in the distribution of energy (intensity) when two or more waves superimpose each other.
To observe interference of light waves, the two sources of light must be coherent with respect to each other.
If the waves emitted from two sources have the same frequency and constant phase difference, the sources are coherent sources.
In 1801, Thomas Young obtained evidence for the particle nature of light.
Monochromatic light is a particular wavelength from a single source.
When a plane wave passes through a slit, a circular wavelet emerges from a slit.
Constructive interference results in dark fringes.
Destructive interference results in a dark fringe.
For bright fringe to be formed at P, the path difference is equal to an integral number of wavelengths.
For dark fringes, the condition is $d \sin \theta = (m + \frac{1}{2}) \lambda$.
The zeroth order ($m = 0$) bright corresponds to the central bright line.
The first-order brights ($m = 1$) can be seen only on one side of the central line.
Fringe width is defined as the distance between two adjacent bright fringes.
The position of fringes and fringe width depend on the wavelength of light source.
In Young's experiment, the emerging light arrives at the second screen which has two parallel narrow slits $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ separated by a distance $d$.
When path difference is equal to a half-integral number of wavelengths, the waves will interfere constructively.
For $D \gg d$, $\theta \approx \theta'$, where $\theta$ is the angle from original direction of light wave.
The approximation $\theta (\text{rad}) \approx \sin \theta \approx \tan \theta \approx \frac{y}{D}$ is valid when $\theta$ is small.
The distance of $m^{th}$ order bright fringe is $y_{m} = m \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
The distance of $(m+1)^{th}$ order bright fringe is $y_{(m+1)} = (m+1) \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
Fringe width $\Delta y_{m} = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
Light waves reaching the centre of the screen ($\theta = 0$) travel the same distance, so they are in phase.
A bright fringe at centre is called central bright line.
Secondary wavelets spread out only in forward directions with the speed $v$.
The interference pattern consists of a series of bright and dark parallel lines called fringes.
Young's experiment proves that light has wave characteristics.
If phase difference is $0, 2\pi, 4\pi$, these two positions are in phase.
Interference occurs when two waves meet while traveling in opposite media.
The fringe width calculation assumes monochromatic light.
A wavefront is a locus of all points in the wave that have different phases.
Destructive interference results in bright fringes.
Light sources must maintain a constant phase relationship to be coherent.
The angular position of a point of a periodic wave is known as the wave period.
The distance of $m^{th}$ dark fringe from central bright line is $y_{m} = (m + \frac{1}{2}) \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
The zeroth order bright fringe is formed at $\theta = 0$.
The path difference $S_{2}A$ is equal to $d \sin \theta'$.
Interference of light involves the modification in the distribution of energy.
In Young's double slit experiment, bright and dark fringes are formed alternatively.
The intensity of the dark fringe in Young's double slit experiment is maximum.
The position of fringes and fringe width depend on the wavelength of light source.
The distance between the first and second order bright fringes is equal to twice the fringe width.
Diffraction is defined as the bending of waves around the corners of an obstacle or through an aperture.
The amount of bending in diffraction does not depend on the wavelength of light.
If the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light, the bending will be almost unnoticeable.
Light waves diffract around objects and create bright and dark fringes.
Fresnel diffraction and Fraunhofer diffraction are the two main classes of diffraction.
In Fresnel diffraction, the source of light and the screen are at an infinite distance from the obstacle.
In Fraunhofer diffraction, there is an infinite distance between the source and the screen from the obstacle.
When light passes through a single slit for Fraunhofer diffraction, the screen is at a distance $D \gg a$ away from the slit.
The diffraction pattern consists of a dark central portion called the central minimum.
Each successive bright fringe becomes less intense proceeding outward, away from the central maximum.
Secondary wavelets from any two corresponding points of the upper and lower halves of the slit reach the point $P_0$ exactly out of phase.
For the first minimum in a single-slit diffraction, the two waves interfere destructively.
The condition for the first minimum in a single-slit diffraction is given by $a \sin \theta = \lambda$.
For the second minimum, the slit is considered to be divided into four parts.
The path difference between two waves from corresponding points from each quarter of the slit is $\frac{a}{2} \sin \theta$.
Generally for the $m^{\text{th}}$ minimum, $a \sin \theta = m\lambda$ where $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
In the single-slit diffraction minimum condition, $m$ can take the value of $0$.
The width of the central maximum is equal to the position of the first minimum.
The formula for the width of the central maximum is $2 \frac{\lambda D}{a}$.
A narrower slit causes a wider central maximum in a single-slit diffraction pattern.
Most modern-day applications of slit interference use exactly two slits for practical purposes.
A diffraction grating can disperse light into its wavelength components.
Diffraction gratings are widely used as dispersive elements in spectrographic instruments.
Two types of grating are transmission grating and reflection grating.
A transmission grating can be manufactured by carving glass with a sharp tool in a large number of precisely positioned parallel lines.
The number of lines (or slits) per unit length is called the grating constant.
The condition for maximum intensity in a diffraction grating is the same as that for a Young's double slit.
For maximum intensity to be formed at P, the path difference of two waves from adjacent slits is equal to an odd number of wavelengths.
The path difference between two waves from adjacent slits in a grating is given by $d \sin \theta$.
In the equation $d \sin \theta = m\lambda$, $\theta$ is the angle of diffraction.
If the number of slits per unit length is $N$, then $d$ is equal to $\frac{1}{N}$.
There is a large dispersion of incident white light at the centre where $m = 0$.
In the first order diffraction spectrum, longer wavelength (red light) diffracts at a greater angle than shorter wavelength (violet light).
A compact disc is an example of reflection gratings.
The tracks of a compact disc act as a diffraction grating, producing a separation of the colors of white light.
Interference of light waves is defined as the modification in the distribution of light energy.
